metadata.description: as_completed: The `asyncio.as_completed(fs)` function yields futures in the order they complete. The first future to complete will be yielded first. Here is an example of how you might use this in Python: ```python import asyncio # Define some asynchronous tasks async def task1(): await asyncio.sleep(1) return 'Task 1 completed' async def task2(): await asyncio.sleep(2) return 'Task 2 completed' async def main(): # Create a list of futures representing the tasks fs = [task1(), task2()] # Use as_completed to run and yield results in the order they complete for coro in asyncio.as_completed(fs): result = await coro print(result) # Run the main function asyncio.run(main()) ``` In this example, `task1` completes before `task2`, so "Task 1 completed" will be printed first.

data_pointers.title: as_completed
data_pointers.content_preview: as_completed: The `asyncio.as_completed(fs)` function yields futures in the order they complete. The first future to complete will be yielded first. Here is an example of how you might use this in Python: ```python import asyncio # Define some asynchronous tasks async def task1(): await asyncio.sleep(1) return 'Task 1 completed' async def task2(): await asyncio.sleep(2) return 'Task 2 completed' async def main(): # Create a list of futures representing the tasks fs = [task1(), task2()] # Use as_completed to run and yield results in the order they complete for coro in asyncio.as_completed(fs): result = await coro print(result) # Run the main function asyncio.run(main()) ``` In this example, `task1` completes before `task2`, so "Task 1 completed" will be printed first.
